items.title: Text spam detection with linear SVM
items.scenario: A team trains a linear SVM on 20k emails with TF-IDF features (spam = +1, ham = −1). After tuning C via cross-validation, the model keeps 312 support vectors from the training set. A new email is classified by sign(wᵀx + b); score magnitude reflects confidence in separation, not a probability.
items.explanation: High-dimensional sparse text is a classic linear SVM use case. Support-vector sparsity means inference uses hundreds of critical emails, not the full 20k training corpus.

items.title: Medical image patch screening
items.scenario: Radiology patches are labeled benign/malignant. Data is noisy — some benign patches resemble malignant ones. Hard-margin SVM fails to converge; soft-margin SVM with C = 0.1 yields a wider margin tolerating borderline cases, while C = 10 fits training labels tightly but overfits artifacts.
items.explanation: Soft margin C directly controls the bias-variance trade-off when classes overlap. Cross-validation picks C that generalizes on held-out scans.

items.title: Credit risk with overlapping features
items.scenario: Income and debt-ratio features separate most applicants, but a cluster of high-income defaulters overlaps low-risk applicants. The SVM places a hyperplane maximizing margin while allowing slack on overlapping points; support vectors include both margin-touching good loans and misclassified defaulters.
items.explanation: Support vectors are not only points on the margin boundary — violators inside the margin can also become support vectors when slack is active.

items.title: Handwritten digit pair (1 vs 7)
items.scenario: Binary SVM on flattened pixel vectors separates digit 1 from 7. Only ~5% of training pixels act as support vectors. Removing random non-support training images does not change the boundary; removing one support vector shifts the hyperplane.
items.explanation: Demonstrates sparsity: the decision function depends on a subset of training data, unlike k-NN which needs all stored points at query time.
